text: Hello and welcome to another episode of This is My Architecture. I'm Sonika and today I have
Saket from Tally with me. Hello Saket. Hi Sonika, thank you for having me here today. Thanks for
giving us your time Saket. Can you tell us a bit about Tally? Yeah, Tally is the business
management software that has enabled 7 million users across 2 million small and mid-sized
businesses across the world for their accounting, inventory and payroll functions. During the
last 35 years we've been in the business domain, we've always created technology and products
that adapts to the way our customers run their businesses. What is Tally Prime on AWS? Tally
Prime on AWS is based on our current architecture which is Tally Prime. We are taking an enterprise
grade Windows desktop application and putting it on the cloud at scale for our customers.
We're also building our next generation technology architecture which is cloud native and which
will set new industry benchmarks. That's nice. You spoke about 2 million SMBs, right? Yeah.
And that's like a big scale. Yeah. So why don't you talk us, how are you managing that scale
with Tally Prime on AWS? Sure. So our secret ingredient for scale has been this concept of what we call
as the multi-AWS account architecture. We have different types of AWS accounts and as we scale,
we are able to add new AWS accounts with new VMs and scale in an unlimited manner. If I were to draw an
analogy to data center design, then the shift from vertical computing, vertical scaling to horizontal
scaling is the analogy we would see. So earlier people used to have a lot of vertical servers and
now they've shifted to pizza box styled one use servers in the data center racks. Similarly, what we have
here is an account which is what we are referring to as the customer port account. So this is a customer port
account which consists of 1000 VMs. So as we scale, we just add more and more these pizza boxes and we
are able to scale to an unlimited number. So AWS multi-account structure is your secret for scalability. Yeah.
So that's great. Why don't you talk about security because you're using Tally Prime on AWS for accounting
and I'm sure security is important. Absolutely. Security is paramount when it comes to Tally,
customer financial data security and privacy is paramount. So in addition to the standard data at
rest and data in transit, what we have here is the admin account. So this is the admin account.
In the admin account, we have this concept, innovative concept of dual authentication for the customer.
When the user logs in through a web browser or through a thick client, they land up at the login API,
which is effectively API gateway endpoint, which is backed by a Lambda, which would then authorize
the concept of dual authentication is internally will first authenticate. And then it would, if that
authentication is successful, it will send it for a second level authentication to tally.net backend,
where the second level of authentication would happen. And once this is done,
it would return a secret token. So this is in conjunction with the CMK of KMS. If the authentication
is successful, then the security token is returned back to the user and which can then be used to
connect to the customer port account. That's great. So once the user receives the token circuit,
how does the user use the token to use Tally Prime on AWS? Yeah, that's a very interesting question.
So once the token has arrived at the user end, that is passed through Nice DCV. So we are using Nice DCV as
a high performance desktop promoting solution. It's a very important ingredient for the overall
solution because it is zero cost and enterprise support. Additionally, it has the flexibility to
allow us to connect through browsers as well as thick lines. So using a browser or a thick line,
a nice DCV allows us to use either of the two solutions. The customer request is routed to
CloudFront over the internet. At the CloudFront, we are also using Lambda at Edge. So the Lambda at Edge
at CloudFront would then route it to the correct ALB because we have this large scale, right? So we have
multiple ALBs and the ALB would then route it to the right container. So this is effectively a single
ECS cluster running on EC2 and we are then routing and landing at the correct container. Just one thing
to point out about the previous admin account. Once the request has landed to the container,
it would do authentication back with the authorizing Lambda. Oh, so this is a really great journey,
user interaction and then that's how we get Tally Prime on AWS, right? The virtual office to the user.
Yeah. So once the virtual office is there, right? It is also the simplicity, right? And Tally is known
for power of simplicity. Yeah. Can you also talk about how are you managing power of simplicity with
this architecture? Absolutely. So if you forget about all this complexity, right? This technical
mumbo-jumbo, what does the end user care about? So the end user wants a virtual office with the virtual
computers. That is exactly what we're delivering. We are able to deliver the used to working at Tally on
their laptop or desktop. Now we are giving that exact same experience in a virtual computer that is
hosted in the cloud. So the end users and customers don't have to worry about this complexity and they're
getting exactly what they're used to. The same Tally interface, the same features, everything is
identical. Oh, wow. Now we started with 2 million SMB users and that's why Tally Prime on AWS is created.
I'm sure cost is really important, right? Because this is for SMBs. Can you talk about how are you
managing cost with this architecture, Sakit? Yeah, that's an extremely valid, you know,
very important question because we have obsessed very hard about optimizing costs here. We're talking
about SMBs of India, right? We're not talking about large companies. So how do we optimize the costs is
is absolutely essential. In fact, that made the project much more harder to implement. Had it not been the
cost factor, we could have made decisions which were simpler to implement, but we couldn't.
One of the primary ways you've controlled the costs is we're running the entire thing on Linux as
opposed to Windows to control the costs that are associated with software licensing. Additionally,
we're using the Docker containerization using ECS and that allows us to basically
squeeze the most out of the virtual hardware that we've got.
And during the initial design, we chose AMD instances over Intel instances. That had also
helped us with significant savings. Sakit, thank you so much for giving us time today and explaining
how Tally Prime on AWS runs. Thank you for having me, Sonika. Thank you for watching. This is my architecture.
